title: How to Develop Psychic Abilities Through Automatic Writing
meta_description: Discover the truth about your sensitivity. It is not a flaw. It is a doorway. Automatic writing is the simplest key. No tools. No rituals. Just a pen, paper, and your own hand. This article shows you how to unlock your natural psychic ability starting today.
og_title: How to Develop Psychic Abilities Through Automatic Writing
og_description: Discover the truth about your sensitivity. It is not a flaw. It is a doorway. Automatic writing is the simplest key. No tools. No rituals. Just a pen, paper, and your own hand. This article shows you how to unlock your natural psychic ability starting today.
body:
You're Not Broken - You're Just Picking Up Signals Most People Miss

Let me guess. You've always known things you shouldn't know. You've walked into a room and felt the tension before anyone spoke. You've thought of someone and they called five minutes later. You've had dreams that played out exactly in real life. And somewhere along the way, someone told you that you were "too sensitive" or "imagining things."

Here's the truth nobody told you: You're not broken. You're not making it up. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. And that sensitivity? It's the raw material for psychic ability. It's not a curse - it's a door.

One of the oldest, most direct ways to walk through that door is automatic writing. It doesn't require a crystal ball, a special room, or any kind of initiation. All you need is a pen, paper, and the willingness to let your hand move without your brain getting in the way. Right?! That's it. No fancy tools. Just you and the page.

I'm Kalesh. I've been teaching psychic development for over a decade, and I've watched thousands of people open up abilities they didn't know they had through this one practice. So let's get into it.

What Automatic Writing Actually Is (And Isn't)

Automatic writing is when you write without consciously controlling what comes out. Your hand moves, words form, and you're not the one deciding what to say. It's like your pen becomes a receiver, picking up signals from somewhere beyond your everyday thinking mind.

Some people call it channeling. Some call it inspired writing. Some call it getting out of your own way. The label doesn't matter. What matters is the experience.

You're not trying to "become psychic" through automatic writing. You're remembering that you already are. The writing is just the training wheels. It gives your logical mind something to do while the rest of you opens up. Does that land?

Think of it this way: your normal thinking mind is like a radio station playing at full volume. Automatic writing doesn't turn that station off - it just turns it down enough that you can hear the other stations broadcasting underneath. The ones you've been ignoring your whole life.

Why Your Brain Will Fight This (And That's Normal)

The first time you try automatic writing, your brain will scream at you. It will say things like:

  • "You're making this up."
  • "This is just your imagination."
  • "This is stupid. Go do something productive."
  • "You're not doing it right."

Good. That's a sign it's working. Your brain is designed to protect you from the unknown, and automatic writing is deeply unknown territory for most people. The voice that says you're faking it? That's just fear wearing a logic costume.

Here's the thing: you don't need to believe in automatic writing for it to work. You just need to do it. The belief comes later, after you've seen the evidence on the page.

"The voice that says you're making it up is the gatekeeper. Your job isn't to silence it - it's to write past it. Every word you put down while that voice is yelling is a brick you've removed from the wall between you and your knowing."

So when that voice shows up, thank it for trying to protect you, and keep writing. The doubt doesn't mean you're wrong. It means you're on the edge of something real.

Setting Yourself Up for Success: The Practical Setup

Before you start, you need a few things in place. Not because the process is fragile, but because your nervous system needs to feel safe enough to open up.

Your Physical Setup

  • A pen that feels good in your hand. Not too thin, not too thick. One that glides without pressure. Ballpoint, gel, fountain - whatever works for you.
  • Paper you're not afraid to "waste." A cheap notebook is better than a fancy journal because you won't worry about "ruining" it. You need permission to write garbage.
  • A quiet space where you won't be interrupted. Fifteen minutes minimum. Turn off your phone. Close the door. This is your time.

Your Mental Setup

  • No expectations. The fastest way to block automatic writing is to want something specific to happen. You're not trying to contact a specific spirit or get a specific answer. You're just opening the channel and seeing what comes through.
  • No editing. You cannot correct spelling, grammar, or content while you're writing. If you stop to fix something, you've broken the flow. Let it be messy. Let it be wrong. Let it be nonsense. That's how the real stuff gets through.
  • No judging. What comes out might be boring, confusing, or embarrassing. That's fine. You're not showing this to anyone. This is between you and whatever is moving through you.

If you want a structured guide to walk you through the first few sessions, I recommend The Psychic Workbook by Echo Bodine. It's a solid, no-nonsense resource that doesn't try to sell you on anything. Just practical exercises. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Step-by-Step Process: How to Actually Do It

Here's the exact process I've taught to hundreds of students. Follow it as closely as you can for your first few sessions. Once you get the hang of it, you can adapt it to what works for you.

Step 1: Ground Yourself (2 minutes)

Before you pick up the pen, you need to be in your body. Psychic work without grounding is like trying to tune a radio while you're being shaken. It doesn't work well.

Sit comfortably. Put your feet flat on the floor. Take three slow breaths. With each exhale, imagine any tension draining out through your feet into the earth. That's it. You don't need a visualization of roots or light or anything fancy. Just the awareness of your body in the chair and your feet on the ground.

Step 2: Set Your Intention (30 seconds)

Say this out loud or in your head: "I am opening to receive guidance that is for my highest good. I welcome information that comes through clearly and kindly. I release any need to control what comes through."

That's it. You don't need to invoke any specific beings or spirits. Your own higher self is more than enough to start.

Step 3: The Warm-Up (3 minutes)

This is the part most people skip, and it's why most people get frustrated. Your hand needs to move without thinking before it can move without your brain controlling it.

Write the following sentence at the top of your page: "I am allowing whatever wants to come through to come through."

Then keep writing that same sentence over and over. Don't try to vary it. Just let your hand repeat it. After about a minute, you'll notice your handwriting starting to change. It might get looser, bigger, or more slanted. That's the shift happening.

Step 4: Ask a Question (1 minute)

Once you feel your hand moving more freely, stop the repetition and write a question. Keep it simple. Something like:

  • "What do I need to know right now?"
  • "What is blocking me from my clarity?"
  • "What is the next step on my path?"

Don't think too hard about the question. The first one that comes to mind is usually the right one.

Step 5: Write Without Stopping (10-15 minutes)

Here's the rule: your pen cannot stop moving. Even if you're writing "I don't know what to write I don't know what to write this feels stupid" over and over, keep the pen moving. The moment you stop, your logical brain jumps back into the driver's seat.

If you get a word that doesn't make sense, write it anyway. If you get a sentence that sounds like gibberish, write it anyway. If you feel like you're making it all up, write that too. The act of moving the pen without stopping is what opens the channel. The content is secondary.

You'll probably hit a wall around the 3-minute mark. That's the "resistance wall." It's where your brain tries to shut the whole thing down. Push through it. Keep writing. The good stuff usually comes right after you push through that wall.

Step 6: Close the Session (1 minute)

When your time is up, write a clear closing line like "This session is complete. Thank you." Put the pen down. Shake out your hand. Take a deep breath.

Don't read what you wrote yet. Your logical mind is still too close to it. Put the notebook away and come back to it later - at least an hour, ideally the next day.

What to Expect (And What Not to Expect)

Most people's first few sessions look like this:

  • Lots of repetition ("I don't know I don't know I don't know")
  • Random words that don't connect
  • Childish or simple sentences
  • A few phrases that feel surprisingly clear or wise
  • Handwriting that looks nothing like your normal handwriting

That's all normal. The first sessions are about building the habit, not getting perfect messages. You're training your nervous system to trust that it's safe to open up. That takes time.

Some people get clear, coherent messages from the first session. Some people write gibberish for weeks and then suddenly get a flood of clarity. Both are fine. Your only job is to show up and keep the pen moving.

If you want to deepen your practice, I highly recommend You Are a Channel by Laura Day. It's one of the most practical books on channeling and automatic writing I've ever read. No fluff, just exercises. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Tell If It's Really Coming Through (Or If You're Making It Up)

This is the question everyone asks. And it's a good one. Here's how to tell the difference.

When you're making it up: The writing feels effortful. You're thinking about what to write next. You're editing as you go. The words feel like they're coming from your normal thinking voice. You might feel proud of how clever or wise something sounds.

When it's coming through: The writing feels effortless. You're almost watching your hand move. You might be surprised by what shows up on the page. Sometimes you'll read it later and think, "I don't remember writing that." The words might be more direct, less filtered, or even a little blunt. You might not feel particularly "spiritual" while it's happening - it can feel very ordinary.

Here's the secret: it doesn't matter if you're making it up or not. The act of writing without your conscious control opens a door in your mind. Even if the first 90% is your own imagination, that last 10% is real. And over time, that percentage shifts. The channel gets cleaner. You learn to tell the difference because you've practiced it.

Doubt is part of the process. It doesn't mean you're wrong. It means you're human.

Common Blocks (And How to Get Past Them)

Block #1: "Nothing is happening."

This is the most common block. You sit down, you start writing, and it feels like you're just moving the pen yourself. Nothing special. No chills, no voices, no deep wisdom.

The fix: Keep going. The "nothing is happening" feeling is your logical mind's way of saying "this is boring, give up." But the channel is opening whether you feel it or not. You don't need to feel something for it to be working. Trust the process, not the feeling.

Block #2: "I'm just repeating myself."

You write the same phrase over and over. "I don't know what to write I don't know what to write." It feels pointless.

The fix: That repetition is your brain's resistance. It's trying to fill the space with noise so nothing real can come through. Keep writing the repetition until it shifts. It will shift. Usually right when you're about to give up.

Block #3: "I feel silly doing this."

This is a big one. The ego hates looking foolish. And automatic writing can feel very foolish at first.

The fix: Let yourself feel silly. Write about feeling silly. "I feel so stupid doing this this is ridiculous why am I even trying." Get it out on the page. Once you've expressed the embarrassment, it usually dissolves. The ego just wants to be heard.

For more on working through blocks and resistance in psychic development, check out The Awakened Psychic by Kala Ambrose. She does a great job addressing the practical hurdles that come up. See on Amazon (paid link)

Taking It Deeper: Moving Beyond the Basics

Once you've been practicing automatic writing for a few weeks, you can start to play with it more intentionally. Here are some ways to go deeper:

Ask Specific Questions

Instead of "What do I need to know?" try more specific questions like:

  • "What is the root of this recurring pattern in my life?"
  • "What is my soul's purpose for this year?"
  • "What do I need to heal to move forward?"

Specific questions get specific answers. But don't rush to this. Build the foundation first.

Write for Others

Once you feel confident with your own channel, try writing for a friend. Ask them to send you a question mentally (or write it down and seal it in an envelope). Then do your automatic writing session and see what comes through for them. This is a powerful way to test your accuracy and build trust in your abilities.

Combine with Dream Work

Keep your notebook by your bed. Before you sleep, ask a question and intend to receive the answer through writing in the morning. When you wake up, before you do anything else, pick up the pen and write without thinking. This catches the channel when it's still open from the dream state.

Experiment with Different Questions

Not all questions work equally well. Avoid yes/no questions - they tend to produce short, unsatisfying answers. Open-ended questions work best. Also avoid questions that are really demands in disguise ("Tell me my life purpose right now"). Approach the process with curiosity, not desperation.

If you want a complete system for developing your intuition and psychic abilities, The Intuitive Way by Penney Peirce is a classic for a reason. It covers automatic writing alongside other techniques in a grounded, practical way. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Most Important Thing Nobody Tells You

Automatic writing isn't about getting messages from dead people or angels or aliens. It can be, if that's your thing. But at its core, automatic writing is about getting out of your own way.

You already know more than you think you do. You've been picking up on things your whole life - the tension in a room, the truth behind someone's words, the direction your life needs to go. But your logical mind has been editing all of it, filtering it through fear and doubt and "that's impossible."

Automatic writing is just a way to bypass that editor. It's a practice that teaches your nervous system that it's safe to let the knowing through without the filter. And once you learn that, you can access it anywhere - not just with a pen and paper.

So here's my challenge to you: try this for 21 days. Fifteen minutes a day. No skipping. No judging. Just show up and let the pen move. See what happens.

You might be surprised at what's been waiting for you on the other side of your own doubt.

Ready to go deeper? Take our free assessment to discover your dominant psychic ability and get a personalized practice plan. Start the assessment here.
